leadershipThought: Think before you speak. Think before you joke. Think before you act. Ask yourself: if this shows up in front of a client, my family, or leadership — does it represent me well? If the answer is no — don't do it.
opsContent:
Breakroom — Tuesday July 1, 2026

Jobs Completed:
• Robert Wright — SDU Tract complete, heading back (12:10 PM)
• Jordan Lemmons — starting job (3:50 PM)
• James Stephens — Diamondback Mabee 13-24 1-1H finished for the day (6:30 PM)
• Stefanie Kirschke — Ovintiv Tom Ranch released, returning 7:00 AM Wednesday (6:20 PM)
• Ray Solis — University complete, rolled to rig down at H&P 628 (7:18 PM)
• Jacob Hernandez — Bizzel Irvin conversion complete, Co Rep released, moved to HP 616 (7:12–7:29 PM)
• Caleb Smith — Shaw 13-8 #183 finished for the day (7:29 PM)

Carry-Forward into Week 4:
• SM Energy Gipple 154H — 2,000ppm H2S (Dennis Easley) — monitoring and escalation plan required
• Cosmo phase 2 — H2S 3,000ppm / CO 3% (Louie Zarate) — supplied air protocol required before re-entry
• Nobles 3202D — crane absent, rescheduled (Edward Carrillo) — confirm equipment on site before dispatching
lessonsLearned:
• SM Energy Gipple 154H — 2,000ppm H2S (Dennis Easley, July 1 3:51 PM) — multi-week elevated readings; do not dispatch without confirmed monitoring and escalation plan
• Cosmo phase 2 — H2S 3,000ppm / CO 3% (Louie Zarate, July 1 9:00 AM pull) — dual-gas hazard confirmed; supplied air is not optional at this location, it is the plan
• Nobles 3202D — crane not on site (Edward Carrillo, July 1 3:22 PM) — crew stood by waiting on MT orders; job rescheduled; confirm all equipment is on location before crew departs the yard
exxonMoments:
Hold Points Honored, Not Just Documented (Operational Discipline)
A Canon technician documented all hold points across three phases of a job — pre-job, mid-job scope change, and post-job confirmation. The observation: hold points exist because sequential steps create compounding risk. Skipping one to save time does not eliminate the hazard — it removes the last chance to catch it before the next step locks it in. Completing the form and honoring the hold point are not the same thing.

Familiarity Is Not a Substitute for Process (Operational Discipline)
A crew running back-to-back jobs at adjacent locations began treating the second job as a continuation of the first. Task steps were abbreviated because they had just done it. The observation: operational discipline means treating every job as the first time. The scope did not carry over — and neither should the shortcuts. The hazards at job two are not reduced because you just finished job one.

Post-Job Debrief Is Part of the Job (Operational Discipline)
A job was completed and the crew was released, but no post-job debrief was conducted before departure. The OWL form captures what happened — but the debrief captures what we will do differently. Leaving without that conversation means the lesson stays in the form instead of traveling with the crew to the next location.

Documentation Is the Discipline You Leave Behind (Operational Discipline)
A Canon technician logged atmospheric monitoring readings in real time at a high-H2S location — entry reading, mid-job reading, and exit reading, all recorded as they happened. That record does not just protect the crew that was there. It protects every crew that follows. The next person arrives with data, not assumptions. Operational discipline includes the paperwork.

fieldNews.headline: Ray Solis finished at the University and rolled straight to a rig down at H&P 628 the same evening — no gap, no waiting, just moved.
Stefanie Kirschke worked through a cement shut-in delay at Ovintiv Tom Ranch — posted updates all day, confirmed the schedule, and had a clean 7 AM return locked in before she left.
James Stephens put Diamondback Mabee 13-24 1-1H to bed and posted up in Breakroom before heading out. Short message, job done.
Michael Hutt got SM First Tee #121 well opened and back in play — posted the update so dispatch and the team know the location is active.
Robert Wright wrapped the SDU Tract job clean and headed back. Job complete, crew moving — that's all you need to post.
SM Energy Gipple 154H — 2,000ppm H2S. Dennis Easley flagged it Tuesday afternoon. This location is not a walk-up — confirm your monitoring plan before you leave the yard.
Cosmo phase 2 — H2S 3,000ppm and CO 3% on the 9 AM pull. Louie Zarate confirmed both readings. Supplied air is not optional at this location — it is the plan.
Nobles 3202D — crane was not on site. Edward Carrillo made the call: crew stood down, job rescheduled. Confirm your equipment is on location before you dispatch.

todaysChallenge: Before you leave the location today — ask yourself: did I document what actually happened, not just what was supposed to happen?
todaysChallengeSubtext: The OWL form, the monitoring log, the debrief — these are not checkboxes. They are the record that protects the next crew. Fill them out on site, before you drive away.
